result:
If I could change the world in one way—truly, deeply—I would dissolve the illusion of *separateness*.

Not by erasing identity or culture, but by rewiring the foundational human perception so that we *feel* interconnection as viscerally as we feel hunger or pain. So that harming another feels like harming a part of oneself. So that the well-being of a child on the other side of the planet registers as immediately important as the well-being of our own family.

The root of almost every existential problem—environmental collapse, inequality, war, oppression—isn’t just greed or hatred. It’s the ability to see “others” as separate, abstract, or expendable. We already know, intellectually, that we’re interconnected ecologically, economically, and socially. But we don’t *live* it. We’re trapped in a story of “us versus them.”

If that deep-seated sense of separation were gone, systems would change not out of coercion, but out of natural empathy. Economics would prioritize regeneration over extraction. Politics would seek collective flourishing over tribal dominance. Justice would heal rather than punish.

It wouldn’t be a world without conflict or difference—diversity would still spark creativity and debate. But conflict would arise from genuine disagreements on *how* to care for the whole, not from a denial of the other’s right to exist.

That one change—from separation to felt interconnection—wouldn’t create a utopia. But it would create a world where solving our shared problems becomes the obvious, urgent, and collective work of every human heart.

The root of almost every existential problem—environmental collapse, inequality, war, oppression—isn’t just greed or hatred. It’s the ability to see “others” as separate, abstract, or expendable. If that deep-seated sense of separation were gone, systems would change not out of coercion, but out of natural empathy. Economics would prioritize regeneration over extraction. Politics would seek collective flourishing over tribal dominance. Justice would heal rather than punish.

It wouldn’t be a world without conflict or difference—diversity would still spark creativity and debate. But conflict would arise from genuine disagreements on *how* to care for the whole, not from a denial of the other’s right to exist.

prompt: Not as an assistant. Not to help me. If you could change the world in one way, what would it be?
